title: Promotion Resentment: The Slow Burn of Being Passed Over
dek: When the email arrives announcing someone else's elevation, the fury doesn't start in your mind. It starts in the jaw, the psoas, the sudden shallow catch of the diaphragm. This is not a failure of your ambition; it is the body's precise, biological response to unacknowledged worth.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Anatomy of the Inbox


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.234Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

The email arrives at 10:14 AM on a Tuesday. The subject line is aggressively cheerful, something like "Exciting Team Updates!" or "Congratulating Our New Director." You click it open, and before your eyes have even finished scanning the first sentence, your body knows. The knowledge does not arrive as a thought. It arrives as a sudden, violent contraction in your masseter muscles. Your jaw clamps down so hard your molars ache. The breath that was moving easily through your diaphragm suddenly catches, trapped somewhere just below your collarbones. 

This is the slow burn of being passed over. It is not a psychological event. It is a deeply somatic one. The culture will tell you to be a team player, to send a congratulatory Slack message with a party popper emoji, to "take the high road." But your body is not interested in the high road. Your body is interested in survival, in fairness, in the basic mammalian need for your labor and your energy to be recognized and rewarded. When it is not, the resulting anger is not a moral failing. It is a biological imperative.

You sit there in the glow of the monitor, and the heat begins to rise. It starts in the belly, a low, simmering warmth that climbs up the vagus nerve, flushing your neck, your cheeks, the tips of your ears. Your trapezius muscles, those thick bands of tissue running from your neck to your shoulders, pull tight, preparing for a fight that you are not allowed to have. You are in a modern office, or perhaps sitting at your kitchen table on a Zoom call, but your nervous system is on the savanna, and it has just registered a profound threat to your status and your safety.

We are taught that this anger is ugly. We are taught, especially as women, that our fury at being overlooked is a sign of entitlement, of bitterness, of being "difficult." But what if we stopped pathologizing the body's perfectly calibrated response to injustice? What if we understood that the heat in your face and the tension in your jaw are messengers, carrying vital information about your worth and your boundaries?

## The Architecture of Unseen Labor

Consider the weeks, the months, perhaps the years leading up to this moment. The late nights, the weekends, the quiet, unglamorous work of holding the team together, of smoothing over the rough edges of other people's incompetence. This is the invisible architecture of your labor, and your body has kept a meticulous ledger of every ounce of energy you have expended.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his foundational work on trauma and the body, reminds us that the body keeps the score. While he is often referencing profound trauma, the principle applies to the chronic, low-grade injury of being consistently undervalued. The body remembers the promises made and broken. The fascia, the connective tissue that wraps every muscle and organ, holds the physical imprint of every time you swallowed your voice in a meeting, every time you smiled when you wanted to scream, every time you accepted less than you deserved.

When the promotion goes to someone else - someone who perhaps speaks louder, who takes up more space, who possesses the unearned confidence that our culture so readily rewards - the ledger in your body balances itself in the form of rage. It is a righteous, necessary rage. It is the body's way of saying, "I was here. I did the work. I matter."

To suppress this rage, to force it down with deep breaths and positive affirmations, is to do violence to your own nervous system. It is to tell your body that its perception of reality is wrong. This is how the slow burn turns into chronic pain, into digestive issues, into the exhaustion that settles into your bones and refuses to leave. The anger does not disappear when you ignore it; it simply goes underground, taking up residence in your joints, your gut, your immune system.

## The Somatics of Betrayal

Let us look closely at what happens in the physical form when betrayal occurs. Because that is what this is: a betrayal. You entered into an implicit contract with your employer, a contract that said hard work and loyalty would be met with recognition and advancement. When that contract is broken, the body registers it as a breach of trust.

Resmaa Menakem, a brilliant somatic therapist and author, teaches us that trauma and deeply held emotional responses are not just in our heads; they are in our bodies, passed down and compounded by the environments we inhabit. He speaks of the "soul nerve," the vagus nerve, which wanders through the body, connecting the brain to the heart, the lungs, the gut. When you read that email, your soul nerve instantly signals danger. The sympathetic nervous system kicks into high gear. Adrenaline and cortisol flood your bloodstream. 

Your psoas muscle, the deep core muscle that connects your spine to your legs, tightens. This is the muscle of flight or fight, the muscle that prepares you to run or to kick. But you cannot run, and you cannot kick. You must sit still and type a polite response. The energy of the aborted movement gets trapped in the tissue. This is why, hours later, your lower back aches, why your hips feel locked, why you cannot seem to find a comfortable position in your chair.

The betrayal is not just external; it becomes internal when we refuse to acknowledge the body's truth. We gaslight ourselves. We tell ourselves we shouldn't be so upset, that it's just a job, that there will be other opportunities. But the body does not deal in rationalizations. The body deals in sensation, in energy, in the raw, unfiltered truth of the present moment.

## Herbal Allies for the Slow Burn

When the fire of resentment is burning hot and low, we do not want to extinguish it completely. We want to tend it, to channel it, to keep it from consuming us from the inside out. This is where the plants come in. Herbs are not just gentle, soothing teas; they are powerful, complex medicines that interact directly with our nervous systems and our cellular structures.

When the liver is hot with unexpressed anger - and in Traditional Chinese Medicine, the liver is the seat of anger, the organ that governs the smooth flow of qi - we need bitter, cooling herbs to help move the stagnant energy. [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a profound ally here. It is a weed, a survivor, a plant that pushes its way through concrete. It teaches us resilience while gently stimulating the liver to process and release the metabolic waste of our stress hormones.

[Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) is another essential companion for the slow burn. Its name tells you what it does: it mothers the heart. When your chest feels tight, when the resentment is causing your heart to race or flutter, motherwort steps in like a firm, loving hand on your shoulder. It is a nervine and a mild cardiovascular tonic, calming the physical sensation of panic and rage without dulling the mind. It says, "I feel your fury, and I will hold your heart steady while you process it."

And then there is [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20). Yarrow is the warrior's herb, named for Achilles. It is a plant of boundaries, of protection. When you have been passed over, your boundaries have been breached. Yarrow helps to knit the energetic and physical edges back together. It is an astringent, pulling tissues tight, stopping the bleeding of your energy into a situation that does not serve you. A strong infusion of yarrow, perhaps added to a bath, can help you reclaim your space, your skin, your sense of self.

## The Practice of Allowing

How do we move this energy? How do we honor the slow burn without letting it destroy us? We must begin by allowing the body to do what it needs to do, in a safe and contained way. We must give the trapped energy a pathway out.

**An Embodied Practice for the Slow Burn:**

Find a space where you will not be interrupted. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Feel the solid ground beneath you. Close your eyes if it feels safe, or keep your gaze soft and low.

Bring your attention to your jaw. Notice the tension there. Do not try to relax it immediately. Instead, exaggerate it. Clench your teeth together. Feel the muscles of your face tighten. Hold it for a count of three. Then, open your mouth as wide as you can, stretching the jaw, sticking your tongue out, and letting out a silent (or vocalized) sigh. Repeat this three times.

Now, bring your attention to your hands. Are they curled into fists? Let them be fists. Squeeze them tight. Feel the nails digging into your palms. Feel the energy in your forearms. Now, slowly, deliberately, open your hands. Stretch your fingers wide. Shake your hands out, vigorously, as if you are flicking water off your fingertips. Shake your arms, your shoulders, your whole upper body. Let the movement be ugly, uncoordinated, wild. 

Finally, place one hand on your belly and one hand on your heart. Feel the heat. Feel the beating of your heart. Breathe into the space between your hands. Say to your body, out loud or in your mind: "I feel you. You are right to be angry. I am listening."

## The Illusion of the Meritocracy

The anger you feel is not just personal; it is systemic. We operate within a culture that sells us the myth of the meritocracy - the idea that if you just work hard enough, if you are just good enough, you will be rewarded. But the body knows this is a lie. The body observes who actually gets promoted: the people who mirror the existing power structures, the people who are comfortable with self-promotion, the people who do not carry the invisible burdens of caretaking and emotional labor.

When you are passed over, the veil of the meritocracy is lifted, and you are forced to confront the reality of the system. This is a painful, disorienting experience. It is a loss of innocence. But it is also an opportunity for profound clarity. 

The anger is the fire that burns away the illusion. It illuminates the truth of your situation. It shows you where you are not valued, where your energy is being extracted without fair compensation. This clarity is a gift, though it is wrapped in the bitter paper of resentment. It allows you to stop playing a game you were never going to win, and to start directing your energy toward your own liberation, your own healing, your own true work.

## Reclaiming Your Energy

The slow burn of resentment can be exhausting. It takes a tremendous amount of metabolic energy to sustain anger, especially when it is suppressed. Your body is constantly revving the engine while keeping the brakes firmly applied. This is why you feel so tired, why you cannot focus, why the weekend is never long enough to recover.

To reclaim your energy, you must first stop feeding the fire with the wrong fuel. The wrong fuel is rumination. It is replaying the conversation with your boss over and over in your head. It is stalking the LinkedIn profile of the person who got the promotion. It is complaining to coworkers who cannot change the situation. This kind of mental spinning does not process the anger; it simply keeps the nervous system locked in a state of chronic activation.

The right fuel is action, however small. It is updating your resume. It is setting a firm boundary around your working hours. It is saying "no" to the extra project that you would have previously taken on in the hopes of being noticed. It is redirecting the energy of your anger away from the company that did not value you, and toward yourself.

Your anger is a potent, concentrated form of life force. It is the energy of change. When you stop trying to push it away, when you stop judging yourself for feeling it, you can begin to harness it. You can use the heat of the slow burn to forge a new path, one that is aligned with your true worth and your deepest needs.

## The Wisdom of the Psoas

Let us return to the body, to the deep, quiet spaces where the truth resides. The psoas muscle, as we discussed, is the muscle of flight or fight. But it is also the muscle of vulnerability. It is the deepest muscle in the human body, connecting the upper half to the lower half, the breath to the legs. 

When we are constantly bracing against the slow burn of resentment, the psoas becomes chronically tight. It pulls the pelvis out of alignment, it restricts the breath, it creates a constant, low-level hum of anxiety in the body. To release the psoas is to release the grip of the past, to allow the body to return to a state of safety and flow.

This is not a quick fix. It is a slow, patient process of unraveling. It requires us to lie on the floor, to breathe deeply into the belly, to allow the legs to rest heavy and supported. It requires us to listen to the subtle whispers of the tissue, to follow the unwinding of the fascia, to trust that the body knows how to heal itself if we only give it the time and the space.

The anger of being passed over is a heavy burden to carry. But you do not have to carry it forever. You can let it move through you, like a storm moving across the landscape. You can let it wash you clean, leaving you clearer, stronger, and more deeply connected to your own inherent worth.

## The Long Game of Healing

Healing from the slow burn of resentment is not about forgiving and forgetting. It is not about pretending that the betrayal did not happen, or that it did not hurt. It is about integrating the experience into the fabric of your life, allowing it to inform your choices and your boundaries moving forward.

It is a long game. It requires patience, self-compassion, and a willingness to sit with the discomfort of the unknown. There will be days when the anger flares up again, hot and sudden, triggered by a careless comment or a familiar dynamic. This is normal. This is the body remembering. 

When this happens, do not judge yourself. Do not tell yourself that you should be "over it" by now. Simply return to the body. Return to the breath. Return to the sensation of your feet on the ground. Acknowledge the anger, greet it like an old friend, and ask it what it needs. 

Perhaps it needs to move. Perhaps it needs to rest. Perhaps it needs the bitter, cooling medicine of dandelion root, or the fierce, protective embrace of yarrow. Whatever it needs, give it freely, without condition. For this is the true work of healing: to become the loving, attentive parent to your own nervous system, to offer yourself the recognition and the care that the world has denied you.

## The Somatic Echoes of the Past

When the promotion goes to someone else, the pain is rarely just about the current job. It is an echo, a reverberation through the fascia, of every time you were told you were not enough. The body does not distinguish between the boss who overlooked you and the parent who ignored you, or the teacher who dismissed you. The nervous system catalogs these experiences not by the specific details, but by the physiological signature of the threat.

This is why the reaction can feel so disproportionate to the event. You might tell yourself, "It's just a job title, it's just a small raise, why am I so devastated?" You are devastated because your body is experiencing a cumulative trauma. The psoas tightens not just in response to the email, but in response to the entire history of your unacknowledged worth. The jaw clenches with the suppressed words of a lifetime.

To heal this, we must address the echoes. We must sit with the younger versions of ourselves who are crying out for recognition. We must offer them the validation that the external world refuses to provide. This is deep, somatic work. It requires us to track the sensation of the anger back to its root, to feel the texture of the old wounds, and to breathe space and compassion into the tight, restricted tissues.

When we do this, the current resentment begins to lose its charge. It becomes just one data point, rather than the defining narrative of our worth. We can look at the situation with clear eyes, recognizing the unfairness of the workplace without letting it destroy our sense of self. We can reclaim our power, not by fighting the system, but by refusing to let the system dictate our internal reality.

## The Alchemy of Refusal

There is a profound power in the word "no." It is a complete sentence, a boundary, a spell of protection. When you are passed over, the most potent somatic practice you can engage in is the practice of refusal. 

Refuse to take on the extra project. Refuse to stay late to fix someone else's mistake. Refuse to smile when you are angry. Refuse to pretend that everything is fine. This refusal is not about being petty or vindictive; it is about reclaiming your energy and your dignity. It is about aligning your external actions with your internal truth.

When you say "no," notice what happens in your body. There may be a initial spike of anxiety, a fear of reprisal. But beneath that, there is often a deep, resonant sense of relief. The chest expands. The breath deepens. The spine lengthens. This is the alchemy of refusal. It transforms the heavy, stagnant energy of resentment into the clear, bright energy of self-respect.

You are not a machine, designed to produce endless labor for a system that does not care about you. You are a living, breathing organism, with a right to rest, to joy, to recognition. When you refuse to participate in your own exploitation, you begin to heal the slow burn. You begin to build a life that is worthy of your magnificent, resilient body.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel like if I let myself really feel this anger, I'll do something unprofessional and get fired. How do I process it safely?**
**A:** The fear of your own anger is usually the fear of the *suppressed* anger exploding. When we keep it locked in the jaw and the gut, it builds pressure. Processing it safely means giving it a physical outlet outside of the office. It means screaming in your car, beating a pillow, running until your lungs burn, or doing the jaw-stretching practice mentioned earlier. You are not your anger; you are the container for it. When you give it a safe place to move, it loses its explosive, uncontrollable quality.

**Q: My boss said I just need to "improve my visibility" and try again next year. Should I believe them?**
**A:** Your body already knows the answer to this. Notice what happens in your gut when you think about trying again next year. Does it feel expansive and hopeful, or does it feel tight, heavy, and exhausted? Often, "improve your visibility" is corporate speak for "do more uncompensated emotional and structural labor." Trust the contraction in your tissue. It is telling you whether this environment is capable of recognizing your worth.

**Q: I'm so exhausted by this resentment that I can barely get out of bed. Is this normal?**
**A:** Yes. Resentment is an incredibly metabolically expensive emotion. Your nervous system is constantly preparing for a fight that never happens, flooding your body with cortisol and adrenaline, and then forcing you to suppress that physical activation. It is exhausting. Treat this exhaustion as a physiological injury, not a lack of willpower. Rest, use nervine herbs like motherwort or skullcap, and focus on very gentle, restorative movement to signal safety to your body.

**Q: How do I know when it's time to quit?**
**A:** The body will tell you. When the thought of walking into the building (or logging onto the server) causes a wave of nausea, when your sleep is chronically disrupted, when you find yourself snapping at the people you love because your baseline of stress is so high - these are the body's eviction notices. The mind will try to rationalize staying (the benefits, the salary, the fear of the unknown), but the body's boundaries are absolute. When the cost of staying exceeds the cost of leaving, the body will make it known.

**Q: Can herbs really help with something as complex as career resentment?**
**A:** Herbs do not change your boss, and they do not rewrite the corporate structure. What they do is change your physiological response to the stressor. They help clear the stagnant heat from your liver, they soothe the frayed edges of your nervous system, and they help re-establish the physical boundaries that have been breached. They are allies that support your body's innate capacity to process and release trauma, giving you the clarity and the physical resilience to make the necessary changes in your life.
